text: The surgical team observes no intraperitoneal adhesions, with the transplant kidney securely positioned in the retroperitoneal space. Ports are placed under direct vision to protect the graft, and the robotic system is docked. The procedure begins with releasing adhesions of the sigmoid colon. Instruments are inserted under visual guidance to avoid graft injury. Posterior dissection proceeds in a standard manner, identifying bilateral vas deferens and dissecting seminal vesicles to the posterior bladder neck. The bladder drop starts from the left side to prevent damage to the transplant kidney and ureter. The peritoneum is incised lateral to the medial umbilical ligament, developing the retropubic space. The right-sided dissection is performed cautiously to protect the transplant ureter, with denser perivesical fat slowing the process and minimal bleeding observed.

The surgeon performs dissection lateral to the prostate, carefully preserving periureteric soft tissue near the transplant ureter. The bilateral endopelvic fascia are exposed and incised, followed by division of the anterior bladder neck. The catheter is delivered, and the posterior bladder neck division is completed. Bilateral vas deferens and seminal vesicles are delivered, and the lateral pedicles are clipped and divided.

The surgeon completes the remaining steps of the procedure in a standard fashion, bags the specimen, and performs left-sided extended pelvic lymph node dissection. Right-sided pelvic lymph node dissection is avoided to prevent injury to the graft vessels and ureter. A two-layer vesicourethral anastomosis is completed.
